sections.title: Definition of interest in securities
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes an 'interest in securities' for regulatory purposes, including ownership, contractual rights, and trust arrangements. It specifies that an interest exists if a person has authority to dispose of or control the disposal of securities, securities-based derivatives, or units in collective investment schemes, regardless of whether the authority is formal or informal. The definition also excludes certain interests like bare trusts and lending-related interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This definition clarifies what constitutes an interest in securities, which is critical for determining data transfer obligations when dealing with financial instruments across borders.

requirements.text: A person has an interest in securities if they have authority to dispose of or control disposal of securities, derivatives, or units

sections.source_quote: A person has an interest in securities, securities-based derivatives contracts or units in a collective investment scheme, if the person has authority (whether formal or informal, or express or implied) to dispose of, or to exercise control over the disposal of, those securities, securities-based derivatives contracts or units in a collective investment scheme, as the case may be.

sections.title: Conditions for approval of exchanges and market operators
sections.summary: This section outlines grounds on which the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) may refuse to approve a Singapore corporation as an approved exchange or recognize a foreign corporation as a recognized market operator. It specifies criteria including criminal convictions for fraud or dishonesty, financial instability, lack of proper qualifications, failure to meet operational standards, and other circumstances that could impair the entity's ability to operate a fair market. These conditions apply to corporations seeking to operate organized markets in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions that must be met for a market operator to be approved, directly impacting cross-border data transfers by requiring assessments of the entity's financial health, operational integrity, and compliance with local standards before data can be processed across borders.

requirements.text: The Authority may refuse approval of a Singapore corporation as an approved exchange or recognition of a foreign corporation as a recognized market operator based on criminal convictions for fraud or dishonesty involving actions that were fraudulent or dishonest.
The Authority may refuse approval based on the corporation being wound up or dissolved.
The Authority may refuse approval if a receiver or receiver and manager has been appointed for the corporation.
The Authority may refuse approval if the corporation fails to satisfy that it is a fit and proper person or that all officers, employees, and substantial shareholders are fit and proper persons.
The Authority may refuse approval if the corporation does not meet the financial standing or business conduct criteria.
The Authority may refuse approval if the corporation's record of past performance or expertise is inadequate for the market it operates.
The Authority may refuse approval if there are other circumstances likely to lead to improper conduct or reflect discredit on the market operations.
The Authority may refuse approval if the corporation does not operate a fair, orderly and transparent market.
The Authority may refuse approval if the corporation does not meet the criteria prescribed under section 10 to be approved as an approved exchange or recognized as a recognized market operator.
The Authority may refuse approval if it would be contrary to the public interest to approve or recognize the corporation.

sections.source_quote: The Authority may refuse to approve a Singapore corporation as an approved exchange, or recognise a Singapore corporation or foreign corporation (as the case may be) as a recognised market operator, on any of the following grounds without giving the corporation an opportunity to be heard: (a) the corporation is in the course of being wound up or otherwise dissolved, whether in Singapore or elsewhere; (b) a receiver, a receiver and manager or a person in an equivalent capacity has been appointed, whether in Singapore or elsewhere, in relation to, or in respect of, any property of the corporation; (c) the corporation has been convicted, whether in Singapore or elsewhere, of an offence committed before, on or after 8 October 2018, involving fraud or dishonesty or the conviction for which involved a finding that it had acted fraudulently or dishonestly.

sections.summary: The Authority may intervene in approved exchanges or recognised market operators that are insolvent, unable to meet obligations, or have contravened the Act. This includes requiring immediate actions, appointing statutory advisers or managers, or assuming control of their business. The Authority must ensure the operator's business is managed in the public interest and investor protection objectives are met. The intervention applies to Singapore-based operations and assets.

sections.summary: The Authority must serve transfer reports to both transferors and transferees. The Authority may require information or documents for its duties. Failure to comply with information requests or providing false information constitutes an offence with fines up to $200,000 and imprisonment. Claiming information might incriminate the person makes it inadmissible in criminal proceedings. A transferor must apply to court for approval of business transfers to transferees. The transferor must lodge reports, obtain Authority consent, publish notices, and maintain copies for 15 days. The Authority and affected parties have rights to appear before the Court. The Court cannot approve transfers without Authority consent. The Court may approve transfers with or without modifications. Transferees not approved as exchanges may have conditional transfers. Orders may transfer business, shares, legal proceedings, dissolve entities, and provide for affected persons. Transfers must be registered with authorities for land. Foreign law compliance may be required for transfers. Transferors and transferees must lodge orders with registries within 7 days. Contravention of this requirement is an offence with fines up to $2,000 and daily penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires court approval for cross-border business transfers with specific reporting, consent, and publication obligations. Transfers must comply with foreign legal requirements and be registered. The Authority has significant oversight powers over transfer processes.

sections.title: Licensing requirements for foreign trade repositories
sections.summary: Foreign trade repositories must apply for licenses from the Authority. Applications require specific forms, fees, and information. The Authority may impose conditions on activities, reported transactions, and investor types. The Authority may vary conditions or impose new ones. Licensees must satisfy conditions throughout the license period. The Authority may refuse licenses based on financial requirements, cooperation with foreign regulators, comparable supervision, and various grounds including criminal history, financial standing, and operational risks. Applicants have the right to be heard before refusal. Licensees must pay annual fees.

The definition applies to licensed trade repositories and their key personnel.

sections.title: Disqualification of directors based on criminal history and financial misconduct
sections.summary: A licensed trade repository must not permit an individual to act as its director or executive officer without prior written consent from the Authority if the individual has been convicted of fraud-related offenses, is an undischarged bankrupt, has enforcement orders against them, has entered into compromised creditor arrangements, has active prohibitions, or has been a director of a regulated financial institution that has been wound up or had its license revoked. The Authority may direct removal of such individuals and provide opportunities for appeal.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict disqualification criteria for directors based on criminal history and financial misconduct, requiring trade repositories to verify the fitness of personnel before appointments and enabling the Authority to remove unfit individuals. This affects DTIA assessments for personnel vetting and governance.

sections.summary: This section defines the objectives of Part 3 of the Securities and Futures Act, which are to promote safe and efficient clearing facilities and reduce systemic risk. sections.title: Definition of associate for regulatory purposes
sections.summary: This section defines who constitutes an associate for regulatory purposes under Singapore's Securities and Futures Act. sections.source_quote: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — 'enforce' means enforce through criminal, civil or administrative proceedings; 'enforcement' means the taking of any action to enforce a law or regulatory requirement against a specified person, being a law or regulatory requirement that relates to the securities and derivatives industry of, or financial benchmarks in, the foreign country of the regulatory authority concerned; 'foreign country' means a country or territory other than Singapore; 'investigation' means an investigation to determine if a specified person has contravened or is contravening a law or regulatory requirement, being a law or regulatory requirement that relates to the securities and derivatives industry of, or financial benchmarks in, the foreign country of the regulatory authority concerned; 'material' includes any information, book, document or other record in any form whatsoever, and any container or article relating thereto; 'regulatory authority', in relation to a foreign country, means an authority of the foreign country exercising any function that corresponds to a regulatory function of the Authority under this Act; 'supervision', in relation to a regulatory authority, means the taking of any action for or in connection with the supervision of — (a) a person operating an organised market, an intermediary or any other person regulated by the regulatory authority; (b) the issuance of or trading in capital markets products in the foreign country of the regulatory authority; or (c) a person administering a financial benchmark, or providing information in relation to a financial benchmark, in the foreign country of the regulatory authority.

sections.title: Revocation of trustee appointments during regulatory control
sections.summary: When the Singapore Financial Services Authority (MAS) assumes control of an approved trustee's business, any prior appointment of chief executive officer or director is deemed revoked unless MAS explicitly approves the person's continued role. MAS may revoke such approvals at any time and the appointment is deemed revoked upon written notice. Any person acting after revocation is guilty of an offence with penalties including fines up to $100,000 and imprisonment for up to 3 years.

sections.title: Notice requirements for trust meetings
sections.summary: A meeting under subsection (3) must be summoned by written notice at least 21 days before the meeting to each participant or joint participant's first named participant in records. Alternatively, the notice must be published in at least four local daily newspapers in English, Malay, Chinese and Tamil languages, also at least 21 days prior. Written notice must be delivered to individuals or their household members, or left at residences, or sent by post. Post delivery is deemed service once stamped and posted. Proof of service requires showing the envelope was properly addressed, stamped and posted. If a resolution passes to wind up the scheme by a majority representing three-fourths in value of participants present and voting, the responsible person and trustee must take necessary steps to wind up the scheme. Contravention of these provisions incurs fines up to $50,000.

requirements.text: Regulations must align practices between Singapore and foreign jurisdictions for dual-listing arrangements
Regulations must enable access to larger liquidity pools and wider range of international investors
Acts must be defensible in both Singapore and foreign jurisdictions

sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Part is to enable regulations to be made to modify the application of this Act and subsidiary legislation made under this Act in relation to the following matters, for the purposes in subsection (2): (a) offers or intended offers of capital markets products that are to be listed on a Dual Listing Board; (b) offers or intended offers of capital markets products that are made using an offer information statement, being offers or intended offers made, or treated under section 239AA as made, by a Dual Listing Board issuer; (c) acts occurring within or outside Singapore in relation to capital markets products listed or to be listed on a Dual Listing Board.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory alignment for cross-border dual-listing

sections.heading: Where the Authority provides information, whether in bulk or otherwise, to any
sections.title: Liability for magnetic tape errors
sections.summary: The Authority and its officers are not liable for errors or omissions in magnetic tapes or electronic information provided to persons if the errors occur in good faith and during the ordinary course of duty. This provision limits liability for data transfers via magnetic tapes or electronic means.

requirements.text: The Authority and its officers are not liable for errors or omissions in magnetic tapes or electronic information provided to persons

sections.source_quote: Where the Authority provides information, whether in bulk or otherwise, to any person by way of magnetic tapes or by any electronic means, neither the Authority nor any of its officers or authorised agents involved in the provision of such information shall be liable for any loss or damage suffered by that person by reason of any error or omission of whatever nature appearing therein or however caused if made in good faith and in the ordinary course of the discharge of the duties of such officers or authorised agents.

sections.heading: [Repealed by Act 24 of 2003]
sections.title: Court orders to restrict financial transfers and capital markets products
sections.summary: This section grants courts the power to issue orders prohibiting payments, transfers, or movements of moneys, capital markets products, or other property when an investigation or proceeding is underway under the Securities and Futures Act. The orders can restrict debt payments, asset transfers, cross-border movements of funds, or property transfers outside Singapore. The court may also appoint receivers for property and impose passport restrictions on individuals. These orders apply to relevant persons including individuals, corporations, and entities covered by the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Court-ordered restrictions on cross-border transfers of financial assets and capital markets products could impact data transfer impact assessments by creating temporary barriers to international financial flows and requiring compliance with specific jurisdictional conditions for data movement.

requirements.text: Court may prohibit payment in total or partial discharge of debt due to relevant person
Court may prohibit payment, transfer or parting with possession of assets to relevant person
Court may prohibit taking or sending moneys of relevant person out of Singapore
Court may prohibit taking, sending or transfer of capital markets products from Singapore to outside Singapore
Court may appoint receiver for property of relevant person
Court may require individual to deliver passport to court
Court may prohibit individual from leaving Singapore without consent

sections.source_quote: A court may, on an application by the Authority, make one or more of the orders referred to in subsection (1A), where — (a) an investigation is being carried out in relation to any act or omission by a person, being an act or omission that constitutes or may constitute a contravention of this Act; (b) a criminal proceeding has been instituted against a person for an offence under this Act; or (c) a civil proceeding has been instituted against a person under this Act, and the court considers it necessary or desirable to do so for the purpose of protecting the interests of any person to whom the person referred to in paragraph (a) or (b) or this paragraph (called in this section the relevant person) is liable or may become liable to pay any moneys, whether in respect of a debt, or by way of penalties, damages or compensation or otherwise, or to account for any capital markets products, or other property.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border financial transfers restricted by court orders

sections.title: District court jurisdiction over securities offences
sections.summary: This section states that despite provisions in the Criminal Procedure Code 2010, a District Court has jurisdiction to try offences under the Securities and Futures Act and can impose full penalties for such offences. The section establishes that the court can handle cases involving violations of the Act, including offences related to capital markets products, business rules, and designated benchmarks. This jurisdiction applies to relevant persons such as approved exchanges, clearing houses, and licensed entities.

sections.title: Cross-border enforcement of Singapore securities offences
sections.summary: This section establishes that a person committing an act partly in and partly outside Singapore that would constitute an offence if done wholly in Singapore is guilty of that offence as if done entirely within Singapore. It also covers acts outside Singapore with substantial and foreseeable effects in Singapore that would be offences under specified parts of the Act. The offences are treated as committed within Singapore for jurisdictional purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by establishing jurisdictional rules for cross-border enforcement of Singapore securities offences, requiring assessments to consider whether foreign activities have substantial effects in Singapore.

requirements.text: A person committing an act partly in and partly outside Singapore that would constitute an offence if done wholly in Singapore is guilty of that offence as if done entirely within Singapore

sections.source_quote: Where a person does an act partly in and partly outside Singapore which, if done wholly in Singapore, would constitute an offence against any provision of this Act, that person shall be guilty of that offence as if the act were carried out by that person wholly in Singapore, and may be dealt with as if the offence were committed wholly in Singapore.
